--- title: "Reproducible examples: a love letter" subtitle: "The best way to answer your own questions" author: "Martin Frigaard" date: "2026-03-05" categories: [R, Python, Markdown] # image: "img/image.png" toc: true toc-depth: 5 toc-title: 'Contents' toc-location: "left" # code-block-border-left: true code-block-bg: "#f8f8f8" code-block-border-left: "#e8e8e8" code-fold: show code-summary: 'show/hide' callout-icon: false draft: false freeze: true knitr: opts_chunk: fig-dpi: 320 fig-width: 6 out-width: '85%' fig-format: png fig-align: 'center' execute: echo: true message: false warning: false eval: false --- ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} :::: {.callout-important collapse='false' appearance='default' icon=false} ## [DRAFT!]{style='font-weight: bold; font-size: 1.15em;'} ::: {style='font-size: 1.10em; color: #282b2d;'} This post is currently under development--thank you for your patience. ::: :::: ::: With developers using LLMs for coding questions, are reproducible examples (reprexes) still relevant? It might seem easier just to ask an AI, but I believe reprexes remain valuable—and we should keep finding ways to make them better. Most people write a reprex to ask about a problem. Sometimes it's an error or warning, but often the code just gives the wrong result. With LLMs generating code, these mistakes are even easier to miss—they may not show up until you test the output or something else fails. Problems without errors or warnings are worse—they look like they work, but no one knows what the code does or if it’s correct. ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} :::: {.callout-tip collapse='false' appearance='default' icon=false} ## [Packages for reproducible examples]{style='font-weight: bold; font-size: 1.15em;'} ::: {style='font-size: 1.10em; color: #282b2d;'} - The [`reprex` package](https://reprex.tidyverse.org/) is designed to '*to encourage the sharing of small, reproducible, and runnable examples on code-oriented websites*.' - The [`reprexpy` package](https://reprexpy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) is a similar package for Python. ::: :::: ::: Let's consider the following example: *We wrote code, executed it, and an error was returned* ```{mermaid} %%| eval: true %%| echo: false %%{init: {'theme': 'neutral', 'themeVariables': { 'fontFamily': 'monospace', 'fontSize':'18px'}}}%% graph TD WriteCode(["Write Code"]) ExecCode(["Execute Code"]) WriteCode --> ExecCode ExecCode --> ErrPath{"Error
Returned?"} ErrPath -.->|"Yes"| Reprex(["Reproducible
Example"]) ErrPath -->|"No"| ResultCheck{"Expected
Result?"} ResultCheck -->|"Yes"| AllGood(["Expected
Output"]) ResultCheck -->|"No"| SilentFail["Silent Failure
Wrong result,
no error"] ResultCheck -->|"Unknown"| NobodyKnows["Unverifiable
No one knows what
it should produce"] %% ErrPath -.-> Reprex(["Reproducible Example"]) style NobodyKnows fill:#D32F2F,color:#fff style SilentFail fill:#ffa500,color:#fff style AllGood fill:#388E3C,color:#fff style Reprex fill:#4051B5,color:#fff ``` ## Embracing minimalism The real art in creating a good reprex is identifying the code that isn't needed to reproduce the error (including packages, function calls, objects, etc). Deleting code can be painful, especially if you've already made multiple attempts to address the error. But winnowing down code gives us an opportunity to think clearly and deeply about the problem we've encountered. ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} :::: {.callout-tip collapse='false' appearance='default' icon=false} ## [Revision = open-mindedness]{style='font-weight: bold; font-size: 1.15em;'} ::: {style='font-size: 1.10em; color: #282b2d;'} > '*rewriting is the key to improved thinking. It demands a real open-mindedness and objectivity.*' ::: :::: ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} :::: {.callout-tip collapse='false' appearance='default' icon=false} ## [Revision = clarity]{style='font-weight: bold; font-size: 1.15em;'} ::: {style='font-size: 1.10em; color: #282b2d;'} > '*[revision] demands a willingness to cull verbiage so that ideas stand out clearly. And it demands a willingness to meet logical contradictions head on and trace them to the premises that have created them.*' ::: :::: ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} :::: {.callout-tip collapse='false' appearance='default' icon=false} ## [Revision = courage]{style='font-weight: bold; font-size: 1.15em;'} ::: {style='font-size: 1.10em; color: #282b2d;'} > '*In short, it forces a writer to get up his courage and expose his thinking process to his own intelligence.*' - Marvin H. Swift, [Clear Writing Means Clear Thinking Means…](https://hbr.org/1973/01/clear-writing-means-clear-thinking-means) ::: :::: :::
